From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Leader of a collaborative work comparable to the author of a book

For the band, see [The Auteurs](/wiki/The_Auteurs "The Auteurs").

An ***auteur*** ([/oʊˈtɜːr/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); French: [[otœʁ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French"), lit. '[author](/wiki/Author "Author")') is an artist with a distinctive approach, usually a [film director](/wiki/Film_director "Film director") whose filmmaking control is so unbounded and personal that the director is likened to the "author" of the film,[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantas200218-1) thus manifesting the director's unique style or thematic focus.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMinJooKwak200385-2) As an unnamed value, auteurism originated in French [film criticism](/wiki/Film_criticism "Film criticism") of the late 1940s,[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaughie201322–34,_62–66-3) and derives from the critical approach of [André Bazin](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Bazin "André Bazin") and [Alexandre Astruc](/wiki/Alexandre_Astruc "Alexandre Astruc"), whereas American critic [Andrew Sarris](/wiki/Andrew_Sarris "Andrew Sarris") in 1962 called it **auteur theory**.[[4]](#cite_note-Britannica-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

American actor [Jerry Lewis](/wiki/Jerry_Lewis "Jerry Lewis") directed his own 1960 film *[The Bellboy](/wiki/The_Bellboy "The Bellboy")* via sweeping control, and was praised for "personal genius". By 1970, the [New Hollywood](/wiki/New_Hollywood "New Hollywood") era had emerged with studios granting directors broad leeway. [Pauline Kael](/wiki/Pauline_Kael "Pauline Kael") argued, however, that "auteurs" rely on creativity of others, like cinematographers.[[6]](#cite_note-:1-6)[[7]](#cite_note-:2-7) [Georges Sadoul](/wiki/Georges_Sadoul "Georges Sadoul") deemed a film's putative "author" could potentially even be an actor, but a film is indeed collaborative.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESadoulMorris1972-8) [Aljean Harmetz](/wiki/Aljean_Harmetz "Aljean Harmetz") cited major control even by film executives.[[9]](#cite_note-:3-9) [David Kipen](/wiki/David_Kipen "David Kipen")'s view of the screenwriter as indeed the main author is termed *[Schreiber theory](/wiki/Schreiber_theory "Schreiber theory")*. In the 1980s, large failures prompted studios to reassert control. The auteur concept has also been applied to non-film directors, such as [record producers](/wiki/Record_producer "Record producer") and [video game designers](/wiki/Video_game_designer "Video game designer"), such as [Hideo Kojima](/wiki/Hideo_Kojima "Hideo Kojima").[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Notable examples of filmmakers throughout history frequently cited as auteurs include [Wes Anderson](/wiki/Wes_Anderson "Wes Anderson"), [Christopher Nolan](/wiki/Christopher_Nolan "Christopher Nolan"), [Ava DuVernay](/wiki/Ava_DuVernay "Ava DuVernay"), [Agnes Varda](/wiki/Agnes_Varda "Agnes Varda"), [Alice Guy-Blaché](/wiki/Alice_Guy-Blach%C3%A9 "Alice Guy-Blaché"), [Sofia Coppola](/wiki/Sofia_Coppola "Sofia Coppola"), [Lars Von Trier](/wiki/Lars_Von_Trier "Lars Von Trier"), [Baz Luhrmann](/wiki/Baz_Luhrmann "Baz Luhrmann"), [Hayao Miyazaki](/wiki/Hayao_Miyazaki "Hayao Miyazaki"), [Guillermo Del Toro](/wiki/Guillermo_Del_Toro "Guillermo Del Toro"), [Francis Ford Coppola](/wiki/Francis_Ford_Coppola "Francis Ford Coppola"), [Adam Sandler](/wiki/Adam_Sandler "Adam Sandler"), [Michael Bay](/wiki/Michael_Bay "Michael Bay"), [Robert Eggers](/wiki/Robert_Eggers "Robert Eggers"), [Tim Burton](/wiki/Tim_Burton "Tim Burton"), [Ari Aster](/wiki/Ari_Aster "Ari Aster"), [Martin Scorsese](/wiki/Martin_Scorsese "Martin Scorsese"), [Paul Thomas Anderson](/wiki/Paul_Thomas_Anderson "Paul Thomas Anderson"), [Bong Joon Ho](/wiki/Bong_Joon_Ho "Bong Joon Ho"), [the Coen brothers](/wiki/The_Coen_brothers "The Coen brothers"), [Quentin Tarantino](/wiki/Quentin_Tarantino "Quentin Tarantino"), [Jacques Tati](/wiki/Jacques_Tati "Jacques Tati"), [Ingmar Bergman](/wiki/Ingmar_Bergman "Ingmar Bergman"), [Stanley Kubrick](/wiki/Stanley_Kubrick "Stanley Kubrick"), [David Lynch](/wiki/David_Lynch "David Lynch"), [Akira Kurosawa](/wiki/Akira_Kurosawa "Akira Kurosawa") and [Edgar Wright](/wiki/Edgar_Wright "Edgar Wright") .

## Film

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auteur&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Film")]

### Origin

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auteur&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Origin")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Truffaut-BeauSerge.jpg/250px-Truffaut-BeauSerge.jpg)](/wiki/File:Truffaut-BeauSerge.jpg)

Film director and critic [François Truffaut](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Truffaut "François Truffaut") in 1965

Even before auteur theory, the director was considered the most important influence on a film. In Germany, an early film theorist, [Walter Julius Bloem](/wiki/Walter_Julius_Bloem "Walter Julius Bloem"), explained that since filmmaking is an art geared toward popular culture, a film's immediate influence, the director, is viewed as the artist, whereas an earlier contributor, like the screenwriter, is viewed as an apprentice.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBloem1924-11)[[12]](#cite_note-:0-12)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] [James Agee](/wiki/James_Agee "James Agee"), a leading film critic of the 1940s, said that "the best films are personal ones, made by forceful directors".[[12]](#cite_note-:0-12) Meanwhile, the French film critics [André Bazin](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Bazin "André Bazin") and [Roger Leenhardt](/wiki/Roger_Leenhardt "Roger Leenhardt") described that directors, vitalizing films, depict the directors' own worldviews and impressions of the subject matter, as by varying lighting, camerawork, staging, editing, and so on.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThompsonBordwell2010381–383-13)

### Development of theory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auteur&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Development of theory")]

As the [French New Wave](/wiki/French_New_Wave "French New Wave") in cinema began, French magazine *[Cahiers du cinéma](/wiki/Cahiers_du_cin%C3%A9ma "Cahiers du cinéma")*, founded in 1951, became a hub of discourse about directors' roles in cinema. In a 1954 essay,[[14]](#cite_note-certain_tendency-14) [François Truffaut](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Truffaut "François Truffaut") criticized the prevailing "Cinema of Quality" whereby directors, faithful to the script, merely adapt a literary novel. Truffaut described such a director as a *metteur en scene*, a mere "stager" who adds the performers and pictures.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThompsonBordwell2010382-15) To represent the view that directors who express their personality in their work make better films, Truffaut coined the phrase "la politique des auteurs", or "the policy of the authors".[[16]](#cite_note-auteurism-16) He named eight writer-directors, [Jean Renoir](/wiki/Jean_Renoir "Jean Renoir"), [Robert Bresson](/wiki/Robert_Bresson "Robert Bresson"), [Jean Cocteau](/wiki/Jean_Cocteau "Jean Cocteau"), [Jacques Becker](/wiki/Jacques_Becker "Jacques Becker"), [Abel Gance](/wiki/Abel_Gance "Abel Gance"), [Max Ophüls](/wiki/Max_Oph%C3%BCls "Max Ophüls"), [Jacques Tati](/wiki/Jacques_Tati "Jacques Tati"), and [Roger Leenhardt](/wiki/Roger_Leenhardt "Roger Leenhardt"), as examples of these "authors".[[14]](#cite_note-certain_tendency-14)

[Jerry Lewis](/wiki/Jerry_Lewis "Jerry Lewis"), an actor from the Hollywood studio system, directed his own 1960 film *[The Bellboy](/wiki/The_Bellboy "The Bellboy")*. Lewis's influence on it spanned business and creative roles, including writing, directing, lighting, editing, and art direction. French film critics, publishing in *[Cahiers du Cinéma](/wiki/Cahiers_du_Cin%C3%A9ma "Cahiers du Cinéma")* and in *[Positif](/wiki/Positif_(magazine) "Positif (magazine)")*, praised Lewis's results. For his *[mise-en-scene](/wiki/Mise-en-scene "Mise-en-scene")* and camerawork, Lewis was likened to [Howard Hawks](/wiki/Howard_Hawks "Howard Hawks"), [Alfred Hitchcock](/wiki/Alfred_Hitchcock "Alfred Hitchcock"), and [Satyajit Ray](/wiki/Satyajit_Ray "Satyajit Ray"). In particular, [Jean-Luc Godard](/wiki/Jean-Luc_Godard "Jean-Luc Godard") credited Lewis's "personal genius" for making him "the only one in Hollywood doing something different, the only one who isn't falling in with the established categories, the norms, the principles", "the only one today who's making courageous films".[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### Popularization and influence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auteur&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Popularization and influence")]

As early as his 1962 essay "Notes on the auteur theory", published in the journal *[Film Culture](/wiki/Film_Culture "Film Culture")*,[[18]](#cite_note-18) American film critic [Andrew Sarris](/wiki/Andrew_Sarris "Andrew Sarris") translated the French term *la politique des auteurs*, by [François Truffaut](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Truffaut "François Truffaut") in 1955, into Sarris's term *auteur theory*. Sarris applied it to Hollywood films, and elaborated in his 1968 book, *[The American Cinema: Directors and Directions 1929–1968](/wiki/Andrew_Sarris "Andrew Sarris")*, which helped popularize the English term.

Via auteur theory, critical and public scrutiny of films shifted from their stars to the overall creation.[[12]](#cite_note-:0-12) In the 1960s and the 1970s, a new generation of directors, revitalizing filmmaking by wielding greater control, manifested the [New Hollywood](/wiki/New_Hollywood "New Hollywood") era,[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-time.com-20) when studios granted directors more leeway to take risks.[[21]](#cite_note-21) Yet in the 1980s, upon high-profile failures like *[Heaven's Gate](/wiki/Heaven%27s_Gate_(film) "Heaven's Gate (film)")*, studios reasserted control, muting the auteur theory.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

### Commercial and blockbuster auteurs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auteur&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Commercial and blockbuster auteurs")]

While the term "auteur" is commonly applied to [highbrow](/wiki/Highbrow "Highbrow") and [critically acclaimed](/wiki/Film_criticism "Film criticism") directors, there are examples of commercial filmmakers with a distinctive style who have been labelled as auteurs. Director and producer [Michael Bay](/wiki/Michael_Bay "Michael Bay"), for instance, was described by Peter Suderman, from *[Vox](/wiki/Vox_(website) "Vox (website)")*, as a "subversive cinematic auteur". According to Suderman, "few filmmakers are as stylistically consistent as Bay, who recycles many of the same shots, editing patterns, and color schemes in nearly all of his films", which have the commonality of heavy use of [special effects](/wiki/Special_effects "Special effects"), [computer-generated imagery](/wiki/Computer-generated_imagery "Computer-generated imagery") and explosions, down to its color palettes and filters, which retain a pattern of "neon color contrasts (especially teal and orange)" while "his movies often appear to take place in a perpetual magic hour, with moody sunsets and sunrises looming in the background". As such, despite the mixed and negative reviews of many of Bay's films, Suderman sums up his filmmaking in being consistently "big, loud, and dumb", which makes him a "subversive auteur".[[23]](#cite_note-23)

Another example of an auteur of commercial films is American actor, comedian and filmmaker [Adam Sandler](/wiki/Adam_Sandler "Adam Sandler"), who, according to the Bijou Film Board of the [University of Iowa](/wiki/University_of_Iowa "University of Iowa"), "has curated a catalog of comedies that bear a signature arguably as recognizable as that of an auteur director", whose point of focus of his filmography is "the value of family and friends". In fact, according to Ethan McGuire from *[The Dispatch](/wiki/The_Dispatch "The Dispatch")*, even when "he was neither a husband nor a father", he "was reflecting seriously on what that should mean". Along with being a director and producer, Sandler also is an example of an actor auteur, who has starred on his films alongside character actors who are known for being recurringly cast in his movies, such as [Kevin James](/wiki/Kevin_James "Kevin James"), [Allen Covert](/wiki/Allen_Covert "Allen Covert"), and [Jonathan Loughran](/wiki/Jonathan_Loughran "Jonathan Loughran").[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25)

### Criticism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auteur&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Criticism")]

[Pauline Kael](/wiki/Pauline_Kael "Pauline Kael"), an early critic of auteur theory,[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28) debated Andrew Sarris in magazines.[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[7]](#cite_note-:2-7) Defending a film as a collaboration, her 1971 essay "[Raising Kane](/wiki/Raising_Kane "Raising Kane")", examined [Orson Welles](/wiki/Orson_Welles "Orson Welles")'s 1941 film *[Citizen Kane](/wiki/Citizen_Kane "Citizen Kane")*, asserting extensive reliance on co-writer [Herman J. Mankiewicz](/wiki/Herman_J._Mankiewicz "Herman J. Mankiewicz") and on cinematographer [Gregg Toland](/wiki/Gregg_Toland "Gregg Toland").[[30]](#cite_note-30)

[Richard Corliss](/wiki/Richard_Corliss "Richard Corliss") and [David Kipen](/wiki/David_Kipen "David Kipen") argued that a film's success relies more on screenwriting.[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-corliss-obit-33) In 2006, to depict the screenwriter as the film's principal author, Kipen coined the term *[Schreiber theory](/wiki/Schreiber_theory "Schreiber theory")*.

To film historian [Georges Sadoul](/wiki/Georges_Sadoul "Georges Sadoul"), a film's main "author" can also be an actor, screenwriter, producer, or novel's author, although a *film* is a collective's work.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESadoulMorris1972-8) Film historian [Aljean Harmetz](/wiki/Aljean_Harmetz "Aljean Harmetz"), citing classical Hollywood's input by producers and executives, held that auteur theory "collapses against the reality of the [studio system](/wiki/Studio_system "Studio system")".[[9]](#cite_note-:3-9)

### Law

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auteur&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Law")]

In some law references, a film is treated as artwork while the auteur, as its creator, is the original copyright holder. Under [European Union law](/wiki/European_Union_law "European Union law"), largely by influence of auteur theory, a film director is considered the film's author or one of its authors.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKamina2002153-34)

## Popular music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auteur&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Popular music")]

See also: [Art pop](/wiki/Art_pop "Art pop") and [Recording studio as an instrument](/wiki/Recording_studio_as_an_instrument "Recording studio as an instrument")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/1/1d/Phil_Spector.jpg)](/wiki/File:Phil_Spector.jpg)

Record producer [Phil Spector](/wiki/Phil_Spector "Phil Spector") in 1964

The references of auteur theory are occasionally applied to musicians, musical performers, and music producers. From the 1960s, record producer [Phil Spector](/wiki/Phil_Spector "Phil Spector") is considered the first auteur among producers of popular music.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEisenberg2005103-35)[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBannister200738-36) Author Matthew Bannister named him the first "star" producer.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBannister200738-36) Journalist [Richard Williams](/wiki/Richard_Williams_(journalist) "Richard Williams (journalist)") wrote:

> Spector created a new concept: the producer as overall director of the creative process, from beginning to end. He took control of everything, he picked the artists, wrote or chose the material, supervised the arrangements, told the singers how to phrase, masterminded all phases of the recording process with the most painful attention to detail, and released the result on his own label.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliams200315–16-37)

Another early [pop music](/wiki/Pop_music "Pop music") auteur was [Brian Wilson](/wiki/Brian_Wilson "Brian Wilson"),[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEdmondson2013890-38) influenced by Spector.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECoganClark200332–33-39) In 1962, Wilson's band, [the Beach Boys](/wiki/The_Beach_Boys "The Beach Boys"), signed to [Capitol Records](/wiki/Capitol_Records "Capitol Records") and swiftly became a commercial success, whereby Wilson became the first pop musician credited for writing, arranging, producing, and performing his own material.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEButler2012225-40) Before the "[progressive pop](/wiki/Progressive_pop "Progressive pop")" of the late 1960s, performers typically had little input on their own records.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWillis2014217-41) Wilson, however, employed the studio like an instrument,[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECoganClark200332–33-39) as well as a high level of studio control[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller1992193-42) that other artists soon sought.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEdmondson2013890-38)

According to *[The Atlantic](/wiki/The_Atlantic "The Atlantic")*'s Jason Guriel, the Beach Boys' 1966 album *[Pet Sounds](/wiki/Pet_Sounds "Pet Sounds")*, produced by Wilson, anticipated later auteurs, as well as "the rise of the producer" and "the modern pop-centric era, which privileges producer over artist and blurs the line between entertainment and art. [...] Anytime a band or musician disappears into a studio to contrive an album-length mystery, the ghost of Wilson is hovering near."[[43]](#cite_note-Guriel2016-43)
